attributes.stem:
A 44-year-old man, known to have HIV, has been admitted to the Emergency Department after experiencing a new-onset seizure. The generalised tonic-clonic seizure occurred for three minutes and was accompanied by post-ictal confusion. He began experiencing intermittent fever four days ago, which has been accompanied by headaches and vomiting. He is not on anti-retroviral treatment.

On examination, his temperature is 38.1°C, pulse 96/min, and blood pressure 130/76 mmHg. He appears alert, but his mucous membranes are dry. A CT scan of the head shows multiple ring-enhancing lesions with surrounding oedema at the basal ganglia. His CD4 count is measured at 88 u/L.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Neurological complications significantly affect a substantial proportion of individuals infected with HIV. As immune function progressively deteriorates, the brain and nervous system become susceptible to an array of opportunistic infections and tumours. Toxoplasmosis is the most prevalent CNS infection in AIDS patients, contributing to approximately 50% of cerebral lesions. This condition is brought about by the protozoan Toxoplasma gondii.

In immunocompetent individuals, toxoplasmosis is generally a self-limited and asymptomatic illness. In the context of HIV infection, symptomatic disease most frequently arises due to the reactivation of latent infection, particularly among those with a CD4 count <100 cells/μL. Patients with cerebral toxoplasmosis typically present symptoms such as headaches, confusion, and fever. Focal neurological deficits and seizures are commonly observed. On brain imaging, toxoplasmosis typically manifests as multiple ring-enhancing lesions surrounded by oedematous tissue, with a predilection for the basal ganglia.

Primary CNS lymphoma is a highly aggressive form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma confined to the CNS that emerges in immunosuppressed patients. It contributes to roughly 30% of cerebral lesions in HIV patients and is associated with the Epstein-Barr virus. Clinical signs and symptoms may not differ substantially from those of toxoplasmosis. While CT features can sometimes be mistaken for cerebral toxoplasmosis, HIV lymphoma tends to appear more frequently as a solitary lesion, whereas toxoplasmosis is typically multifocal (86%). Primary CNS lymphoma usually displays subependymal spread.

Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) is characterised by extensive demyelination resulting from the infection of oligodendrocytes by the human polyomavirus (JC virus). Symptoms often manifest gradually over months, including progressive weakness, impaired coordination, and a gradual decline in mental function. Neuroimaging reveals PML lesions as distinct unilateral or bilateral areas of demyelination, displaying neither mass effect nor contrast enhancement.

The focal forms of CNS tuberculosis, including tuberculoma and abscess, are infrequently described. Tuberculomas are notably less frequent compared to toxoplasmosis or primary CNS lymphoma. CT scans typically display a solitary enhancing lesion.

CMV encephalitis is primarily observed in those with a CD4 count <50 cells/μL. The condition may manifest as diffuse micronodular encephalitis or ventriculoencephalitis. The presence of periventricular enhancement and heightened periventricular signal intensity on T2-weighted MRI images suggests CMV ventriculoencephalitis. Ring-enhancing lesions are not typically associated with this condition.
learningPoints: Cerebral toxoplasmosis is a common CNS infection in HIV patients with CD4 count <100 cells/μL, with symptoms such as headaches, confusion, and fever.
Multiple ring-enhancing lesions on CT scanning are characteristic findings in HIV patients with toxoplasmosis.
references: Hogan C, Wilkins E. Neurological complications in HIV. Clin Med (Lond) [Internet]. 2011 Dec [cited 2023 Aug 12];11(6):571. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4952339/ doi: 10.7861/clinmedicine.11-6-571

Basavaraju A. Toxoplasmosis in HIV infection: An overview. Trop Parasitol [Internet]. 2016 Jul [cited 2023 Aug 12];6(2):129. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5048699/ doi: 10.4103/2229-5070.190817
